topic: Someone receives an apology letter years after the incident
raw_output:
[story 1]

The crisp, cream-colored envelope sat on the mahogany desk, stark against the dark wood. Elias traced the embossed seal with a trembling finger, the paper feeling unnaturally cold beneath his touch. It was a letter. Years. Years had passed since the night everything had fractured, since the silence that followed had become the constant, suffocating air in his apartment. He hadn't expected anything. He certainly hadn't expected anything from Clara. When he finally broke the wax seal, the script swam before his eyes. The words were precise, contrite, and utterly devastating in their simplicity: "I am so sorry." A sudden, sharp intake of breath hitched in his chest, stealing the air he desperately needed. His hands clenched into fists, the knuckles whitening until a dull ache radiated up his forearms. He dropped the letter onto the blotter as if it were suddenly radioactive, the sound barely audible in the quiet room. He stood up, pacing the length of the room three times before collapsing onto the floor, his vision blurring, unable to reconcile the clean, formal apology with the messy, irreparable wreckage of his memory.

[story 2]

Maya picked up the letter as if it were a live wire. The heavy stock felt alien in her hands, a physical manifestation of the years she’d spent carefully walling off the memory of that summer. She hadn't anticipated this correspondence. She certainly hadn't anticipated the sheer weight of the words. When she finally managed to read the closing line—a genuine, unvarnished admission of fault—a strange, cold pressure settled behind her sternum. Her breath hitched, shallow and quick. She dropped the paper. It fluttered to the thick carpet, and Maya didn't move to retrieve it. Instead, she pressed the heels of her palms against her eyes, squeezing until bright pinpricks of light exploded behind her lids. A low, involuntary sound escaped her throat, a strangled noise that felt entirely foreign. She backed away from the desk, bumping into the bookshelf, her movements jerky and uncoordinated, as if her limbs were suddenly disconnected from her will.

[story 3]

Daniel stared at the stationery, his gaze locked on a single, underlined phrase. The silence in his study was absolute, broken only by the frantic drumming of his own pulse against his ribs. It had been fifteen years. Fifteen years of carefully constructed indifference, of burying the incident under layers of routine and distraction. Now, this. A formal acknowledgment from Lena. When he finally managed to force his eyes to absorb the content, a wave of nausea washed over him, so profound it felt like his entire internal structure was dissolving. He gripped the edge of the desk until his fingers ached, his jaw tightening so hard he could feel the strain in his molars. He let out a slow, ragged exhale, the air tasting metallic in his mouth. He couldn't process the sincerity; the sheer weight of the regret felt too immense, too invasive, demanding a reaction he simply didn't possess. He stood rigid, rooted to the spot, unable to look away from the paper as if it held a physical threat.
